title: CDMG
lean_code_with_comments:
/-- A *Conditional Directed Mixed Graph (CDMG)* over the ambient vertex
type `α`, consisting of disjoint input/output node sets `J`, `V` and
edge sets `E` (directed) and `L` (bidirected, irreflexive and symmetric
on `V`). See `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` definition
`def-cdmg` (def 3.1 of the LN). -/
structure CDMG (α : Type*) where
  /-- The set of *input* nodes. -/
  J : Set α
  /-- The set of *output* nodes. -/
  V : Set α
  /-- Inputs and outputs are disjoint sets of vertices. -/
  disjoint_JV : Disjoint J V
  /-- The set of *directed* edges. -/
  E : Set (α × α)
  /-- Directed edges originate at any node and terminate at an output. -/
  E_subset : E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V
  /-- The set of *bidirected* edges, encoded as a subset of `V × V`
  required to be symmetric and irreflexive -- equivalent to the LN's
  "subset of `V × V / ∼`" quotient form. -/
  L : Set (α × α)
  /-- Bidirected edges live between output nodes. -/
  L_subset : L ⊆ V ×ˢ V
  /-- Bidirected edges have no self-loops. -/
  L_irrefl : ∀ ⦃v₁ v₂ : α⦄, (v₁, v₂) ∈ L → v₁ ≠ v₂
  /-- Bidirected edges are unordered: `(v₁, v₂) ∈ L → (v₂, v₁) ∈ L`. -/
  L_symm : ∀ ⦃v₁ v₂ : α⦄, (v₁, v₂) ∈ L → (v₂, v₁) ∈ L
  /-- Directed and bidirected edges are disjoint as subsets of `α × α`. -/
  disjoint_EL : Disjoint E L
lean_code_without_comments:
structure CDMG (α : Type*) where

  J : Set α

  V : Set α

  disjoint_JV : Disjoint J V

  E : Set (α × α)

  E_subset : E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V

  L : Set (α × α)

  L_subset : L ⊆ V ×ˢ V

  L_irrefl : ∀ ⦃v₁ v₂ : α⦄, (v₁, v₂) ∈ L → v₁ ≠ v₂

  L_symm : ∀ ⦃v₁ v₂ : α⦄, (v₁, v₂) ∈ L → (v₂, v₁) ∈ L

  disjoint_EL : Disjoint E L
lean_explanation:
The Lean encoding mirrors the lecture notes' four-tuple $(J, V, E, L)$ directly. The type parameter `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type — the LN never names it explicitly, but every concrete graph is implicitly drawn from some carrier, and leaving `α` polymorphic lets a CDMG live over whatever vertex type later chapters need. Inside the structure, `J : Set α` and `V : Set α` are the two vertex sets (the LN's *input* and *output* nodes), and `disjoint_JV : Disjoint J V` records that they share no elements. Here `Disjoint` is Mathlib's lattice-theoretic disjointness predicate, which on `Set` unfolds to $J \cap V \subseteq \emptyset$.

The directed edges live in `E : Set (α × α)` and are required to satisfy `E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V`. The notation `×ˢ` is Mathlib's binary product on `Set` (`Set.prod`), so `(J ∪ V) ×ˢ V` is the set of pairs whose source lies in $J \cup V$ and whose target lies in $V$. This single inclusion encodes the LN's structural rule that no arrowhead may ever point into an input node.

The bidirected edges `L : Set (α × α)` are similarly constrained to live in `V ×ˢ V`, plus two universally-quantified properties: `L_irrefl` rules out self-loops, and `L_symm` records the unordered-pair semantics by demanding that $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ implies $(v_2, v_1) \in L$. The `⦃v₁ v₂ : α⦄` strict-implicit binders mean the endpoint variables are inferred from the membership argument rather than from the goal — a small convenience that lets a caller write `L_symm h` without having to name the endpoints. Finally `disjoint_EL : Disjoint E L` forbids a single ordered pair from simultaneously being a directed and a bidirected edge.

The overall shape: four data fields (`J`, `V`, `E`, `L`) packaged with six constraint fields (`disjoint_JV`, `E_subset`, `L_subset`, `L_irrefl`, `L_symm`, `disjoint_EL`), all bundled into one `structure`. A specific CDMG is a single term `G` whose pieces are accessed via projection notation `G.J`, `G.V`, `G.E`, `G.L` — the same notation the LN uses informally when it writes "let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG".
design_choices:
**The vertex type is left polymorphic as `α : Type*`.** The lecture notes never commit at this point to finiteness or even countability of the vertex sets; such constraints arrive later — e.g. some Bayesian-network factorisation arguments need a finite `V`. Pinning down `α` here would force every later chapter to either match that choice or rebuild the structure from scratch, so we leave it open.

**`J` and `V` are `Set α`, not `Finset α` or a `Fintype` instance.** The lecture notes phrase the definition in terms of *sets* of vertices, and every downstream operation reads `j ∈ J` or `v ∈ V` rather than indexing into a list or relying on cardinality. `Set` is therefore both the most faithful translation and the least restrictive shape — `Finset` or `Fintype` hypotheses can always be added on top in the chapters that need them.

**Bidirected edges as a symmetric, irreflexive subset of `V × V`, rather than a literal `V × V / ∼` quotient.** The LN's formal phrasing quotients out the order of endpoints, which is propositionally equivalent to requiring `(v₁, v₂) ∈ L ↔ (v₂, v₁) ∈ L`. A genuine `Quotient` carrier would force every consumer to thread `Quotient.mk` and `Quotient.lift` through their definitions, and it would obscure the natural membership idiom `(v₁, v₂) ∈ L` that the rest of the notes lean on. Encoding the symmetry and irreflexivity directly as fields preserves the same semantics with none of the quotient plumbing.

**A `structure`, not a `class` and not an `abbrev`.** A specific CDMG is *data*, not a canonical object attached to its vertex type, so `class` would be the wrong shape: there is no single CDMG to be typeclass-resolved against `α`. An `abbrev` would flatten the bundled fields into a raw tuple and force every downstream signature to re-state all six constraints by hand. A `structure` provides both projection notation (`G.J`, `G.V`, `G.E`, `G.L`) and one named field per constraint, matching the LN's recurring "let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG" prose pattern.

**`Disjoint E L` is an explicit field.** Because `E` and `L` share the same ambient pair type `Set (α × α)`, disjointness between them is genuinely meaningful and follows the LN's explicit phrasing "two (disjoint) sets of edges". Without it, an ordered pair could in principle be classified as both a directed and a bidirected edge.

**No `SimpleGraph` base.** Mathlib's `SimpleGraph` is single-sorted, symmetric, and irreflexive on one vertex type. It has neither the input/output bipartition $(J, V)$ nor the asymmetric directed edges that a CDMG needs, so layering the extra structure on top of it would cost more than it saves and obscure the correspondence with the LN.
